t: NICE NG97 — management of vascular risk factors.
Brain MRI (FLAIR — leukoaraiosis, DWI — acute infarcts, T2/SWI — microhemorrhages).
Cardiovascular evaluation — blood pressure, ECG (atrial fibrillation), Holter, carotid USG, echo.
AChEI and memantine — evidence base limited for VaD, but may be effective in mixed dementia; NICE NG97 — considered in mixed cases.
BPSD management — non-pharmacological first; antipsychotics limited (same FDA black box for Alzheimer's).
Rehabilitation after stroke — physical, occupational, speech therapy.
31. — Hypertension, diabetes, lipids, atrial fibrillation, cigarette.
Donepezil, rivastigmine off-label in pure VaD; may be considered in mixed cases.
Risk factor management reduces the rate of decline.
Myth 1: "VaD is completely different from Alzheimer's"
In most dementia cases mixed pathology (Alzheimer + VaD); clinical differentiation sometimes difficult.
Myth 2: "There is no specific treatment for VaD"
vascular risk factor management — first-line and potent intervention; prevention particularly strong.
ASPREE trial (McNeil J.J. NEJM 2018) — primary prophylaxis aspirin did not show evidence as dementia prevention; only in specific stroke prophylaxis indications.
Myth 4: "Vitamins and homocysteine are prophylaxis for vascular dementia"
cognitive prophylaxis with homocysteine-lowering vitamins evidence weak.
Myth 5: "Stepwise decline is mandatory for diagnosing VaD"
Gradual decline typical in small vessel VaD; stepwise only in multi-infarct form.
P. Sachdev et al. Diagnostic criteria for vascular cognitive disorders: a VASCOG statement. Alzheimer Dis Assoc Disord 2014;28(3):206–218.
McNeil J.J. et al. Effect of Aspirin on Disability-free Survival in the Healthy Elderly (ASPREE). NEJM 2018;379(16):1499–1508.
Livingston G. et al. Lancet Commission 2024.
